original_hypothesis.title: update_market Uses Current Block Number for All Historical Epoch Time Calculations, Enabling Retroactive Reward Manipulation
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In LendingLedger.update_market, the epochTime variable is computed using block.number (current block) inside the while loop rather than the iterator variable i (historical block being processed). This means gauge_relative_weight_write is called with the CURRENT estimated timestamp for ALL historical epoch iterations. An attacker can manipulate current gauge weights (via vote_for_gauge_weights) and then trigger update_market on a stale market to retroactively apply favorable gauge weights to all un-updated historical epochs, claiming disproportionate CANTO rewards.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The epochTime formula should use the loop iterator i instead of block.number. All historical epochs receive the same gauge weight as the current period, enabling retroactive weight manipulation for reward theft.

proof_of_signal.pattern: epochTime uses block.number instead of loop variable i; epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000

preconditions: Market has not had update_market called for multiple epochs (stale lastRewardBlock)
Attacker has veCANTO voting power to change gauge weights
cantoPerBlock is nonzero for the relevant epochs

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: epochTime computed with block.number instead of i in while loop iterating from lastRewardBlock to block.number
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: All iterations of the reward accumulation loop use the same current-block epochTime, causing gauge_relative_weight_write to return current weights for all historical periods
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No per-iteration time calculation; should use i instead of block.number

analysis_questions: How many epochs can a market remain stale before someone calls update_market?
What is the maximum reward inflation factor achievable by manipulating current weights?
Does gauge_relative_weight_write return the same value for all loop iterations within one tx?
validation_requirements: Calculate the difference in rewards between using historical vs current gauge weights
Verify that gauge_relative_weight_write returns current-period weight for future epochTime values
Test end-to-end attack: vote, wait for staleness, update_market, claim

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker votes to give a low-weight market 90% gauge weight. Calls update_market on a market stale for 10 epochs. All 10 epochs are credited at 90% weight instead of historical 10%. Attacker's proportional share yields 9x expected rewards.
prerequisites: Deposited position in the target market

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Massive over-distribution of CANTO rewards to attacker's chosen market. Could drain entire LendingLedger CANTO balance.
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All legitimate users across all markets receive fewer rewards
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Difficult — rewards are sent immediately on claim; no clawback mechanism

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying epochTime accuracy per iteration in update_market

technical_investigation_focus.gas_analysis_needed: Gas cost of update_market over many epochs
technical_investigation_focus.capital_requirements: Sufficient veCANTO to shift gauge weights significantly
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Observable on-chain via gauge weight changes before market updates

validation_result.impact_summary: All historical epoch iterations in update_market use the current block's estimated timestamp for gauge weight lookups, allowing retroactive application of current gauge weights to all un-updated historical epochs and enabling reward theft.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The update_market function in LendingLedger.sol iterates from market.lastRewardBlock to block.number, processing rewards for each epoch. However, the epochTime variable (used to query the gauge's relative weight via gauge_relative_weight_write) is computed using block.number on every iteration of the loop, rather than using the loop variable i which represents the historical block being processed. This means gauge_relative_weight_write always receives the same current-block timestamp for every epoch, returning the CURRENT gauge weight for ALL historical periods. An attacker can change gauge weights via vote_for_gauge_weights, then call update_market on a stale market to retroactively apply favorable weights to all un-updated epochs.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 85-87 of LendingLedger.sol clearly show block.number being used instead of i. The loop variable i is defined on line 79 and iterates from market.lastRewardBlock to block.number. The epochTime should use i to estimate the historical timestamp for each iteration, but instead uses block.number which is constant throughout the loop. This is verified by examining gauge_relative_weight_write in GaugeController.sol which passes the time parameter to _gauge_relative_weight, which floors it to the week and looks up points_total[t], points_type_weight[gauge_type][t], and points_weight[_gauge][t].bias at that specific week.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Line 85-87: epochTime is computed as referenceBlockTime + ((block.number - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000. block.number is the CURRENT block number and is constant throughout the entire while loop. It should use the loop iterator variable i instead of block.number to compute the historical timestamp corresponding to the block being processed. This causes all loop iterations to use the same epochTime (current block's estimated timestamp), meaning gauge_relative_weight_write returns the current-period weight for every historical epoch.

calling_sequence: 1. update_market is called with a market address that hasn't been updated for multiple epochs (lastRewardBlock is stale)
2. The while loop begins at i = market.lastRewardBlock (line 79)
3. For each iteration, epoch and nextEpoch are computed correctly based on i (lines 80-81)
4. blockDelta is computed correctly as Math.min(nextEpoch, block.number) - i (line 82)
5. BUG: epochTime is computed using block.number instead of i (lines 85-87), producing the same value on every iteration
6. gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write(_market, epochTime) is called with the constant current-block epochTime (line 90)
7. Inside GaugeController._gauge_relative_weight (line 214-223), the time is floored to week and used to look up current-period weights
8. The result is accumulated into market.accCantoPerShare, applying current weights to all historical blocks
9. On claim() (line 127), the inflated accCantoPerShare results in over-distributed rewards
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entry point is update_market at LendingLedger.sol:72. The loop variable i starts at market.lastRewardBlock (line 79) and increments toward block.number. At each iteration, epochTime is computed on lines 85-87 using block.number (constant) instead of i (varies per iteration). This epochTime is passed to gaugeController.gauge_relative_weight_write on line 90. In GaugeController.sol, gauge_relative_weight_write (line 228) calls _get_weight and _get_total to update state, then calls _gauge_relative_weight (line 214) which floors the time to the week (line 215: uint256 t = (_time / WEEK) * WEEK) and looks up weights at that week. Since all iterations use the same epochTime, all iterations look up the same (current) week's weight. The accumulated accCantoPerShare is then used in claim() (line 129) to compute rewards, resulting in over/under-distribution.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this bug. The defect is in the core loop logic of update_market.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario is valid: (1) Attacker locks CANTO in VotingEscrow to get veCANTO voting power. (2) Attacker deposits into a lending market (directly or via LiquidityGauge) that has low gauge weight. (3) Attacker waits for multiple epochs to pass without update_market being called. (4) Attacker votes heavily for the target market using vote_for_gauge_weights, giving it high relative weight. (5) Attacker calls update_market. All historical epochs are credited with the current high gauge weight instead of the historical low weight. (6) Attacker calls claim to collect the inflated CANTO rewards. The attack is feasible because update_market is permissionless, gauge voting is permissionless, and there is no enforcement that update_market is called regularly.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. This defect allows retroactive manipulation of reward distribution. An attacker can inflate rewards for any market by first changing gauge weights and then triggering update_market on stale markets. The financial impact scales with (1) the number of stale epochs, (2) the magnitude of gauge weight change, and (3) the cantoPerBlock emission rate. In the worst case, an attacker could drain the entire CANTO balance of the LendingLedger contract. All legitimate users across all markets are affected as the total reward pool is finite.
recommended_mitigations: Replace block.number with loop variable i on line 85: uint256 epochTime = referenceBlockTime + ((i - referenceBlockNumber) * averageBlockTime) / 1000;
Consider caching the gauge weight per-epoch to avoid redundant external calls within the same epoch
Add a maximum staleness check to prevent update_market from processing too many epochs in a single call, limiting retroactive manipulation window
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy all contracts. 2. Add a gauge type and add a market as a gauge. 3. Whitelist the market in LendingLedger. 4. Set cantoPerBlock for several epochs. 5. Have user A deposit into the market. 6. Advance blocks through multiple epochs WITHOUT calling update_market. 7. Change gauge weight significantly (e.g., from 10% to 90%). 8. Call update_market. 9. Verify that accCantoPerShare reflects the new 90% weight for ALL historical epochs, not the historical 10%. 10. Compare against a corrected version using i instead of block.number to demonstrate the discrepancy.
uncertainties: The exact magnitude of reward inflation depends on how gauge_relative_weight_write fills historical data vs returns for the queried week - the _get_weight and _get_total calls update state up to the current week, so historical lookups would only return data that was checkpointed

If no checkpoints were ever made in the GaugeController for past weeks, _gauge_relative_weight may return 0 for historical timestamps even with the fix, which means the bug could also cause reward LOSS if current weights happen to be lower than historical weights
additional_testing_needed: Test the interaction between gauge_relative_weight_write state updates and historical lookups to determine exact reward inflation/deflation magnitude
Test behavior when referenceBlockNumber > i (could cause underflow if market was whitelisted before block time parameters were updated)
Measure gas costs of update_market across many stale epochs to determine practical limits on the attack window
